text: Derivation of Equation Describing Diffusion in Salt Flowing Between Parallel Plates 20

Noble-gas daughter-product concentrations $(^{140}\mathrm{Ba},^{141}\mathrm{Ce},^{89}\mathrm{Sr},$ and $^{91}\mathrm{Y})$ were measured in graphite samples removed from the MSRE core after 7800 Mwhr of power operation.
On July 17, 1966, some graphite samples were removed from the MSRE core after 7800 Mwhr of power operation.
Briefly, the graphite samples were rectangular in cross section (0.47 × 0.66 in.) and from 4 l/2 to 9 in. long.

The last section compares computed and measured concentrations of four isotopes $^{140}\mathrm{Ba}$ (from $^{140}\mathrm{Xe}$ ), $^{141}\mathrm{Ce}$ (from $^{141}\mathrm{Xe}$ ), $^{89}\mathrm{Sr}$ (from $^{89}\mathrm{Kr}$ ), and $^{91}\mathrm{Y}$ (from $^{91}\mathrm{Kr}$ ) in the MSRE graphite samples.
It is of interest to point out the difference between this model and a previously derived model used to compute nuclear poisoning from $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ (Ref.
In the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ -migration model, all the xenon that migrates to the graphite comes from the bulk of the salt and is transmitted through the boundary layer.
model in contrast to $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ -migration model, which specifies a long half-life (9.2 hr).

The equation that describes diffusion of a gas in graphite at steady state and includes a decay term is<sup>4</sup>
\frac {\partial^ {2} C _ {g}}{\partial x ^ {2}} + \frac {\partial^ {2} C _ {g}}{\partial y ^ {2}} + \frac {\partial^ {2} C _ {g}}{\partial z ^ {2}} = \frac {\epsilon}{D _ {g}} \lambda C _ {g},
$\lambda =$ noble-gas decay constant $(\mathrm{hr}^{-1})$ $x,y,z =$ coordinates (ft).
\frac {d ^ {2} C _ {g}}{d x ^ {2}} = \frac {\epsilon \lambda}{D g} C _ {g}.
C _ {g} = C _ {g i} e ^ {- x \left(\epsilon \lambda / D g\right) ^ {1 / 2}}. \tag {2}
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} C _ {g}}{\mathrm {d} x}\right) _ {x = 0} = - C _ {g} \mathrm {i} \left(\frac {\varepsilon \lambda}{D g}\right) ^ {1 / 2}.
\operatorname {F l u x} _ {\mathbf {x} = 0} = - \frac {\mathrm {D g}}{\epsilon} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d C g}}{\mathrm {d x}}\right) _ {\mathbf {x} = 0},
\operatorname {F l u x} _ {\mathbf {x} = 0} = \mathrm {C} _ {\mathbf {g} \mathbf {i}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {D} _ {\mathbf {g}} \lambda}{\epsilon}\right) ^ {1 / 2}
C _ {g i} = F l u x _ {x = 0} \left(\frac {\epsilon}{D g \lambda}\right) ^ {1 / 2}, \tag {3}
C _ {g} = F l u x _ {X = 0} \left(\frac {\epsilon}{D g \lambda}\right) ^ {1 / 2} e ^ {- X (\epsilon \lambda / D g) ^ {1 / 2}}, \tag {4}
C _ {g} = \frac {Q}{\lambda} \left(\frac {D _ {S} \epsilon}{D _ {g}}\right) ^ {1 / 2} e ^ {- x (\epsilon \lambda / D _ {g}) ^ {1 / 2}}. \tag {5}

With the boundary condition that at zero time, $C_{g}^{Ba} = C_{g_{0}}^{Ba}$ , the solution is
C _ {g} ^ {B a} = \frac {\lambda^ {X e} C _ {g} ^ {X e}}{\lambda^ {B a}} (1 - e ^ {- \lambda B a t}) + C _ {g _ {0}} ^ {B a} e ^ {- \lambda B a t}. \tag {6}
C _ {g} ^ {B a} = C _ {g _ {0}} ^ {B a} e ^ {- \lambda^ {B a} t}. \tag {7}

With these equations, the $^{140}\mathrm{Ba}$ concentration in the graphite can be determined as a function of time and can be taken through the "reactor on" and "reactor off" cycles by solving the equations the appropriate number of times.

The diffusion coefficient was computed for each sample of each decay chain and the results are shown in column 4 of Table 1.

The diffusion coefficient selected for the remainder of these calculations is shown in the following tabulation, where the values of $D_g^{X_e}$ have been averaged for each sample, and the values of $D_g^{Kr}$ from the $^{89}Kr$ chain were given precedence over the $^{91}Kr$ chain.
In the case of $D_g^{Xe}$ the top and middle samples agree fairly well whereas in the case of $D_g^{Kr}$ the top sample is about 30% of the middle sample.
bNoble-gas generation rate at 7.5 Mw.
cNoble-gas concentration in graphite at surface at 7.5 Mw.
dNoble-gas concentration in salt at interface at 7.5 Mw (in equilibrium with $C_{gi}$ ).

Henry's law constant for Xe in molten salt: $2.75 \times 10^{-9}$ moles of Xe per cc of salt per atm; Henry's law constant for Kr in molten salt: $8.5 \times 10^{-9}$ moles of Kr per cc of salt per atm.

These elements have boiling points of $1238^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $1290^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , respectively, and therefore their vapor pressures could be significant at the reactor operating temperature of approximately $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and they may diffuse a little.
The first significant power operation of appreciable duration started on April 25, 1966, and the graphite sample concentrations were extrapolated back to the sampling date (1100 hr on July 17, 1966).

(5) was evaluated for the noble-gas concentration in the graphite.
The results of these calculations are listed in Table 1 and shown in Figs.
The film thickness for $^{140}\mathrm{Xe}$ , $^{141}\mathrm{Xe}$ , and $^{91}\mathrm{Kr}$ is very thin and the salt velocities this close to the graphite are indeed very low.
In the case of $^{89}\mathrm{Kr}$ , the film thickness is substantial and it is probably coincidental that the model fits this decay chain as well as it does.
Krypton-89 is not a short-lived noble gas in the sense of this development.
As a matter of fact, its half-life (3.2 min) is equivalent to almost eight circuit times of fuel salt around the loop (25 sec).
The $^{89}\mathrm{Kr}$ concentration in the external loop will therefore become appreciable, and effects of the xenon stripper and circulating bubbles as additional krypton sinks must be considered.
Kedl, Development of a Model for Computing the $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ Migration in the MSRE, USAEC Report ORNL-4069, Oak Ridge National Laboratory (to be published).
